title: 2025/12/01 - SSC (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-11-30 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Social Investment Agency [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Social Investment Agency
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Social Investment Agency Hearing of evidence: Social Investment Agency Alistair Mason, Acting Secretary for Social Investment and Chief Executive of the Social Investment Agency Joe Fowler, Deputy Chief Executive, Investment and Commissioning Aphra Green, Deputy Chief Executive, System Performance and Investment Advice
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Social Investment Agency. Social Investment Agency. Alistair Mason, Acting Secretary for Social Investment and Chief Executive of the Social Investment Agency

chunks.text: Um, uh, to the Social Supers and committee, select Committee. So we have, uh, you for 60 minutes this morning. Um, uh, please, uh, feel free to introduce yourselves and, uh, make some introductory, um, comments. And then, uh, after that we'll move to questions from members. I'll try and keep it short. Um, uh, uh, good morning, Mr.

Chair, members of the committee, thank you for the opportunity to present on social investment agencies work over the past year. My name is Alistair Mason. I'm acting Secretary for Social Investment and Chief Executive of the Social Investment Agency. Joining me today is Afra Green, deputy Chief Executive Assistant Performance and Investment Advice. Joe Fowler, deputy Chief Executive Investment and Commissioning.

And behind me have some other members of staff, including our CFA, despite significant and growing investment in social services outcomes for our most vulnerable remain poor. Put simply, people with complex needs are not well served by fragmented and siloed and siloed system in which services are di divided up between different agencies, time and again.

They slip through the cracks. They need integrated solutions delivered early to prevent a cycle of crisis intervention throughout their lives. The role of the SIA is to identify opportunities to break the cycle to ensure people with complex needs received the kind of support they need before the crisis point. To do that, we're using data to see who's at risk of.

We're trusting communities and providers to design solutions, and we're giving them the resources and freedom to deliver. We're collecting and monitoring evidence of what's working and what isn't. In our first year, we have focused on establishing the foundations for complex multifaceted system change with a particular emphasis on data, evidence and targeted investment.

A core part of this work has been opening up access to data and evidence. We've developed a regional data explorer tool that would enable local providers and commissioners to understand population trends and outcomes in their communities. Who needs support, what works, and where the gaps exist. This shifts us towards evidence informed practice at a local level.

Social investment requires clarity about who we're supporting. So we u we are using data to identify cohorts at greatest risk of poor outcomes. We want to understand not just their immediate needs, but the interconnected challenges they're facing. This approach allows us to target interventions where they'll have the greatest impact and track outcomes over time.

As a central agency and a system lead, our role to role is to lead the implementation of the social investment approach, build the infrastructure and tools required, and demonstrate how aspects of social investment can work from practice. Our engagement with other agencies across the social system is already bearing fruit, and we are beginning to see agencies applying the social investment lens to the commissioning programs.

We'll continue to work to shift the system setting standards and using data and evidence so the system invests earlier and more effectively to change lives. Budget 25 a allocated $190 million to the social investment fund over four years. Ministers subsequently decided priority cohorts in August 25.

Round one. Procurement process was openly advertised. That process and foundations we allowed laid down over the 24 25 financial year and now paying off. Most recently, the Minister for Social Investment announced seven organizations working with children and families of complex needs that have, that have been selected to receive funding through Pathway One.

New initiatives together, they stand to receive $50 million over four years, delivering programs to supporting 1600 children and their families per year. Pathway two will consolidate contracts across multiple government departments, reducing the sort of fragmentation that sees providers managing dozens of separate funding streams, spending more time writing reports for Wellington, and less time delivering vital services.

We expect to announce the results of Pathway two process early in the new year. Everywhere else work is well underway, scoping and designing pathways three and four, which involve local commissioning and co-investment with iwi, philanthropists, and community trusts. This year we are shifting from design to delivery, implementing outcome-based contracting, open those partnership pathways, working directly with agencies to demonstrate what social investment looks like in practice.

It's worth emphasizing that while our own efforts amount to only a tiny sum of the overall SP social services spending, our work is about leading and demonstrating an approach to social spending that comes to redefine the way all social services are designed and delivered. With that in mind, we'll continue to bolster our system lead role with guidance and standards to embed social investment principles across the sector.

Ultimately want, we want to put decisions and the information needed to make them as close as possible to the people they affect, and that will be guiding our process in the year to come. Thank you Mr. Chair. We welcome committee these questions. Uh, thank you very much. Uh, first question is from Lawrence. Uh, thank you chair. Um, thank you for coming and thank you for the work that you do.

Uh, my first question is just picking up on something you said where you said Minister decided on priority cohort, how then, because the idea of the agency is a long-term focus and you said outcome focused. Mm-hmm. What happens then when the minister change their mind and then they decide to prioritize something else?

How then would you measure the outcome? Our role is to present the best advice we can to ministers. So they decide the particular cohorts. Um, we implement, uh, the decisions that they make, but our advice is very transparent about how we came across those cohorts, how we selected those cohorts, um, and gave that advice.

They are some of the most vulnerable populations in this country who are likely to have the most long term poor outcomes. Anything you would like to add that Joe? I think only that, um, the, um, cohorts we're investing went into four year arrangements, um, with those organizations to support those changes. Um, the ambition here is that the fund does grow over time and that additional investment in further cohorts would be dependent upon additional funding.

Ultimately, there's a hard edge to the fund. Um, The Your, your investing in in social services, you're looking for the return on those. Um, and continued investment obviously depends upon you having continued confidence in that return. Just a couple of supplementary questions and other people may have subs as well. Um, but in terms of the outcomes, I'm looking also at your, um, non-financial performance indicators in the budget.

Nothing in that financial indicated works about outcomes. It talks about advice that you provide to the thing. So for example, I've seen that, like for example, on page 19 of your report, you mentioned a lot of initiatives that you have taken. Tangible ones. How are you gonna measure the outcomes and what happens when the money that you've allocated for it ran out and you didn't achieve the outcomes you wanted?

Ultimately, you need to use investment logic to test, to see if we're delivering the outcomes that we are expecting to see. We have quite a robust process of the outcomes and the interim indicators that we would expect to see, to see if we're making progress. If it's not working, we need to work with those providers to see if there is a better way we can do to deliver those outcomes.

We need regular measurement and data to see if we are making the most effective, um, or getting the most effective results. Recognizing that these providers are working in some of the most complex situations in the country, um, these results won't come up in a linear progressive way.

They need to have the time and the scope to work with those very vulnerable, um, clients and communities. Uh, so they, they've got the ability to do that, but ultimately we do need to be keeping regular tracking to see if we are making the progress that we would hope to be doing. When we go into a, uh, original contract with a provider, we ask them for it to develop a theory of change that allows that provider to demonstrate to us, and we work alongside them of the, I expect results that they would expect to see with their particular supports that they can provide.

We can then go and measure that to see if that theory of change is actually playing out in reality. Um, that will deliver over time indications that we are making success. And if we're not seeing that success, then it's a, it's a transparent conversation with that provider about what we can do to, to change tech if we're not delivering That. One final supplemental question, if we can just go into one example on page 19.

You mentioned early year support, autism New Zealand. Um, this is hugely important. I agree. Uh, what then would your outcomes be for that? When do you expect to see some form of outcome? And also more importantly, it talks about the needs of autistic children and children selling signs of autism. The barrier that we have now in terms of autism is diagnosis.

Diagnosis is hugely expensive. So is that gonna be also affected? That's addressed in here. I'll ask Joe to address that question. He's been leading this part bit Of work. Yeah, and it's a great example to, to walk through. Thank you. Um, that particular initiative is using some, um, very early screening tools, um, and supporting frontline professionals who work, um, with, with new parents and, and, and very young children, um, to identify people with potentially neurodivergent behaviors at an early age.

Um, they then wrap some extra support around those parents and give them some, um, some, some effectively some tools and techniques that they might wanna consider using that reflect, um, the, the difference and in also in terms of ECE teachers and other people who are engaged with those, with those children. That theory of change that the organization has put forward, um, looks at early identification.

It looks at, um, families and educators better understanding the needs of autistic children and the strategies they can use to have a positive impact, um, in terms of long-term outcomes, um, to decre to decrease child expression of distress and to reduce parental stress and increase parental sense of competence and confidence. Um, ultimately though, um, and this is again a great example, the actual outcomes that you see from that investment are 4, 5, 6 years, hence.

Mm-hmm. You know, you are looking at, um, young children, um, who are in, um, the early stages of school and are more confident and capable and supported in being there than they would've been otherwise if they hadn't had those supports, um, through that, the early phase. So we are looking, that's also an initiative that is quite different and it's testing something new and it's one of the, um, early tests, if you like, of, um, initiatives that might make a wider change to public service and social services across the country in time.

Um, if what they're doing works and has a significant impact and we can clearly see the return on that, then that's something that you wanna see adopted across the Country. Your question, Jen, thank you and thank you for that. Um, I guess reading the annual report, one thing that I felt was missing, which would be understandable in the first year, is the, uh, stakeholder feedback coming through, which I of course can't see any of that, but there has been quite a lot of it outside of your annual report and it's not all been positive.

Um, a UT's recent social transformation research, there was a very strong panel who were very critical of the social investment agency and, um, your approach to social investment, and particularly around the fact that you talk about being disruptors, and I don't disagree with that, but disruption for them looked like six month contracts because people weren't aware of what was going on and the fact that they couldn't get staff and small NGOs shutting down, what's the price to our country if that happens?

And we don't have those people there in the interim until this all takes off. And what do you have to say to that? Um, so some of those smaller providers play a crucial role, um, but we can only fund what we are being mandated to fund. What we need to do is ensure that, uh, in this particular sense that those providers have access to the most appropriate data that they can see, so they can make changes that they can, where they, um, feel is appropriate today, really understand the needs of the community with the data that government has.

We've, um, developed a regional data explor tool, which allows those communities to have far greater access to that data. Um, also the arrangements that we are putting in place take a longer term, uh, look at their contracts so they've got more robustness outside of the social investment agency.

I can't particularly comment, uh, unless Joe, you've got some more to add there. I think only to say That The social investment fund is less than 1% government's investment in, in social services. It wasn't designed to take over or to, um, supplement or in any kind of sequence for funding that was reduced from other organizations and as a, as a result of decisions taken by other agencies.

So I I wasn't quite clear on the Question there. So, so I think that's a really good point that you are making. And we've had this conversation before when I had my briefing, which was great, thank you for that. But that's not what's coming through from other agencies. And these NGOs are getting six month contracts, not able to employ staff because the other agencies are concerned about what's going to happen with procurement because of the social investment agency.

They are saying that, uh, we are not sure where this is going and the social investment. So it is a responsibility of yours and the interactions that you have with those agencies. And we are seeing some of those NGOs collapsing. To your point about, um, the re the data at that same panel, one of the panelists said, I've spent $30,000 on an impact lab report proving that I am making, or my organization is making a difference, but I still cannot get the funding long term now because of this disruption that's happened to the sector

and no one's going beyond six months. Again, we can't really speak for other agencies practice. What we can do is ensure that we're working very closely with those agencies so they understand our practices and what we're up to. The other pathway, which this may be, uh, leading into is the contract consolidation pathway, which is, That was my next question, so I'd love to hear more about that. Thank you.

Which is a pathway where NGOs can apply, um, to, to, um, con consolidate multiple government contracts where it feels most appropriate. They're working with a particular cohort from various different funding agencies. They can then apply to us and work with us to see if there's mechanisms where we can simplify their contracting and put it onto an outcomes based contract, um, and allow for them to work more closely in their communities with those particular cohorts despite, uh, not having to marry up various, um, priorities from various different agencies.

We will do that for on those agencies behalf, um, but it simplifies their reporting and allows them to have far more flexibility over that contract so they can, they can respond to the needs as they serve. Again. Joe, is there anything you want to add in that particular space? He's leading this bit of work? I think, um, I, we can come back to the contract consolidation tool. I just think it's really important to say we've been crystal clear, um, with agencies about our plans for the next year or two and what those investments mean.

Ministers have set us very clear priorities and cohorts that we're investing in. We've also spoken to hundreds of, um, staff and, and leaders from the social sector across NGOs. And again, been really clear about what we're setting out to do. I think I would acknowledge that for many smaller organizations that don't see themselves in social investment fund at the moment in terms of the new investment pathway, but we are seeing, um, some really strong applications come through contract consolidation, and also in the most recent round of funding we've seen it,

um, one collective of smaller organizations that's come through successfully set. Um, yeah. And um, I think that's, that's, that's really encouraging that organizations recognizing that it's difficult to have the backbone around data and insights that you might need, um, to be, um, in that social investment space. Um, but if you club together and think about it collectively and we support, then there's opportunities there.

So, um, just a sub to that, um, Alistair, I wanted to ask about, um, how you have found work with other agencies, um, and how that has tied into, uh, your work and is there good cooperation? Is there an understanding of what you're doing, um, and are they eager and is there help?

Um, along the way? I would say as a, as a general, um, theme that those agencies are very, very supportive and want to lean right into support where we, where they can, where it's appropriate. There's obviously some boundaries that we need to take where, um, where agencies have statutory responsibilities to deliver a particular service.

Ran is a plastic example. Um, but most of those other ones, we need to be very clear that we are not, um, that they're able to perform those particular functions. So those contracts may be, depending on the exact situations, more appropriate to sit back with those host agencies. That will depend very much on the circumstances that it is.

Um, the other thing is, the other aspect is, say, clinical services that probably is far more appropriate to sit with with the, uh, health New Zealand. Uh, rather than us, we're still working through a number of these particular issues and it really does have to be done on a case by case basis, but the cooperation from the agencies has been, um, uh, really outstanding.

Just a, um, and just to follow through, follow up, um, do you, do you have an alignment of cohorts because, um, we've mentioned, uh, uh, identifying cohorts where the focus needs to be on, and do you find that the other agencies have an alignment of, of cohorts with yourselves?

Mm-hmm. Generally, um, there is an alignment of cohorts. You've gotta remember these agencies. They have vertical accountabilities where they've got to look after a particular system. Yes, we are focused more on that, um, horizontal, where we need to look at those cohorts along multiple different ways, looking at their developments over a particular life course or, um, how they react as human beings.

So there's different, there's just different ways of looking at this particular view. Um, our cohorts that we've selected have come from various different mechanisms, but generally we have had expertise in the data from many, many years. And what we've identified is some particular cohorts that keep on appearing as more likely to have very, very poor outcomes.

Mm-hmm. There's many different ways you can cut the data and many different ways you can do that, but this is the first attempt to have a go at looking at this cohort based approach and really taking a different look at it, working closely with the verticals, the vertical agencies along, but taking a more, uh, appropriate sense where we can look, allow those providers to look right across their agencies.

Efer, is there anything you want to mention in the cohort space? Uh, I think that's right. The cohorts enable us to take a cross cutting view of, um, a small population and their service interactions and life experiences over time, and then through the fund to identify, um, how to invest earlier, uh, to get better outcomes for those cohorts.

And then put in place the, uh, continuous feedback loops that enable us to see whether we are achieving what we expected to achieve through those investments with, um, interactions with other agents, with agencies, we are able to see in the data whether, um, and how cohorts are already, um, interacting with those services.

Um, and the, the two have, so the fund and then looking back at agencies will enable us to say, w where could agencies also start to improve over time to get the best outcome from the relatively modest investment through the fund. Thank you. New question, Maureen.

Uh, thank you. Some of it's actually been covered in, in some of the, um, presentation already, so thank you very much for that. Um, I note, I note that, um, with the three demonstration and already underway that it's, um, it's been the opportunity now to bring together, um, some of these programs that would otherwise not have had the funding allocated.

And it was a, it was common feedback from NGOs that the silo based contracting, which had clear KPIs were not always appropriate in every community given what's available. Um, so in terms of, um, us as a committee, uh, reviewing performance of this new way of doing things, given that it's very long term and the, um, we, we review on an annual basis at, at what point are you going to know whether success is in the in site or whether it's time to just, Again, slightly beyond different?

Um, it will depend slightly on each individual program, but overall we need to, the starters, I think, um, the social investment fund has been a missing part of the system architecture for a while, which has been able to take a longer term, but also portfolio neutral view. But some of these particular, um, cohorts, so this is some, so it is, as you say, something new which we need to start from scratch.

We progressively over time need to start reporting on the portfolio of investments that we have. We've, we've got three and we've announced another seven that we are starting to work with. As that gets bigger, this committee will have a strong interest in the, in the overall performance of the fund. There'll be some, some particular investments that, uh, are higher risk and may or may not pay off.

There's be some that are more conservative and we've got far more likely to do that. We'll need to work out our reporting mechanisms to provide a lot of transparency to the committee to show exactly how that's going to work. Um, 'cause I completely agree, there's, there needs to be a depth of understanding from this committee so you can understand exactly what our progress is while also recognizing that earlier statement that I said that some of these things won't take a, necessarily a linear approach to see how the progress is when you're working at that very, um,

vulnerable end of that community. Some we may know success in different ways depending on the individual or the whanau in and in concern. Um, for example, we can't predict in three or four years time how strong engagement with the NGO will come out necessarily. We'll have a good idea based on our theory of change, but it may appear in more regular school attendance.

It may appear in enrollment with the gp, we're not quite sure. Again, those are gonna be individual circumstances, which we need to work out. Uh, we, we are working out a way to report this transparently. So there's, um, allow people like yourselves to take good scrutiny of the work we're doing, um, and also allow us to, um, for our own internal measurement to see if what we're doing is working and if not adjusted.

'cause fundamentally, these, uh, communities need the support that we are providing and they need us get it right. Can I supplement that chair if you don't mind? Just general? 'cause it comes back to a question that I, I'm not sure we quite responded to earlier. And we're currently reviewing, we've got an outcomes framework for the investments since we're investing against those outcomes framework. And the measures we're using for that will, um, through our strategy, be part of our annual measures, um, next year.

And they're in development, which is a question earlier, just in, in terms of the, um, early signs of success. I think you can think of that in, in four stages. The first is actually making an astute investment in the first place. So is the clear theory of change, is it credible? Is there evidence behind it? Do the community support that organization?

Does that organization have the relationships and connections to the public and other social services locally that it's gonna need to be successful? So that initial assessment and based on the, on the cohort and the potential changes that that cohort could achieve with the right support, all of those things come together to, to make a decision. And ultimately what I'll be held to account for as running the fund is that that fund is generating a return that we can clearly see in evidence.

And as we enter the middle stages of that investment, some of those early and intermediate outcomes start to come through. Um, and that starts to give us confidence as to whether that theory is turning out in practice. And again, that enables us to report quarterly to our social investment fund ministers group. And obviously that will enable us to provide richer material to this, to this committee on an annual basis.

Ultimately, the evaluation plan matures on that investment and we get a robust look back at the activities, what's contributed most to the outcomes that we've seen, what can we learn from that evaluation and how do we play that learning back into our new investments rolling forward. So we're trying to get that fund cycle running and based on evidence and learning so that we can make smarter and smart investments over time and help the social sector be as successful as it can be.

Mm-hmm. Yeah. Uh, and it's great to have that on record too, um, you know, for the committee in the future so that, um, yeah, we're all aware of, you know, the challenges and opportunities. Um, one of the things that you've obviously, um, basing, uh, the measurement on is data. And we've had a, we've had a, um, a presentation about the IDI and, but one of the gaps they, uh, referred to was around health and, um, mentioned, um, you know, being enrolled in a GP or whatever.

But, um, at the same time, um, if all of those systems don't talk to each other, uh, then it is difficult to accumulate that data. Is there anything, um, that you've done to maybe help initiate some change in that? I'll let it get into this in a second. But basically, um, there's two aspects to that.

The IDI and more, more data getting into the IDI is incredibly important. Yep. And part of our role as a central agency is start to put pressure on the system for that to happen. Mm-hmm. Um, the second part is data by itself, um, needs to be interpreted and put into context sometimes with that community understanding of what's going on in that particular region and those, um, in those particular, uh, but data can be misconstrued if, if not da, not used thoughtfully.

And we need to be very careful that that can be this, um, that it's done properly. However, the IDI is a is an incredible asset to New Zealand and we can do credible amounts with that. Uh, it is very important if it can probably give you a quick update of the progress that's been made. 'cause considerable amounts of progress has been made over the last year and a lot and a lot, there's a lot of momentum happening in this space.

Yeah. Um, we've worked very closely with Stats New Zealand over the past year and actually building on our longstanding relationship with stats. Uh, so, uh, the, I understand the last data load for the IDI was the biggest ever. Um, and that's probably largely due to a lot of the work that we've done across agencies and to identify missing data sets, uh, that would be helpful for us and, uh, and, uh, for providers and understanding their impact.

So there are 13 new data sets that have gone into the IDI, um, and 18 have been updated, and the frequency of updates, um, will be, um, improved as well. So that, um, you know, it's, as Alistair said, it's not just getting data into the IDI, but it's also giving that data back.

Um, the second part of what we have done over the last year is to, uh, work with providers and with stats to streamline the process for providers themselves to get their data into the IDI. Um, we created, you know, a standardized manual for that, um, and made it easy.

And we have a standard operating practice now with stats as well. Um, and we are now being able to give back that data to providers and to communities in a way, um, as Alistair said, that's, uh, really understandable, and which verified some of their instincts about what they already knew with their services.

Um, and we're able to provide that back to them and say, here's who you're reaching. Here's the impact you're having, here's the other services that the people that you're reaching are interacting with. Um, and, uh, that will, that will enable them to improve the quality of their services. Thank You. I have a question too, if you might bring it here as a supplementary.

Mm-hmm. Um, so in the last couple of years as sort chairing this committee, I've been really interested all hearing all these agencies and, and entities that work in the social sphere. Um, and a lot of talk about we need to focus on outcomes versus outputs do things, a general agreement. That's what needs to happen. What's really difficult, and what I haven't yet heard a clear articulation of is how to actually identify what outcomes are with the complexity of the, you know, um, environments and people and personalities that that's, uh, you know,

that agencies are dealing with. And how to the format, what an outcome is and over a sort of a, a particular period of time as well. So I'm, I'm interested in what you are doing. If you sort of articulated a bit, Joe, you know, there's a bit of the framework, there's theory of change and how you're gonna map against that over time. I'm sort of, how, how are you going look, gonna look at this differently and how are you going to use data and how are you gonna encourage other entities to collate data to then dashboard

for decision makers go Okay, are actually achieving outcomes. Uh, It's a couple of things. Um, but I'll allow after to speak to this. 'cause we've done a lot of work in this space, and I think we also need to remember that perfect is the enemy of God in, in this particular place, because we're working our way through this, we need to do this progressively and we'll learn as we go ahead.

Mm-hmm. Um, but we have, uh, using, we've, we've developed a methodology, um, which has allowed us to steer some of our, where we put a figure on some of our investments and what we'd expect to achieve. And it, um, the, this allows us to, uh, basically it's a cost benefit analysis, but we do that prior to the effect.

And we put do this into the IDI using, uh, two different cohorts of, um, uh, pop two population cohorts in the IDI one is the one that we expect to see. And one is a very, like for like the IDI, this is one of the beauties of the IDI, it's, it's such a large database we can find, uh, a relatively similar population in that database.

Doesn't have to be, um, absolutely perfect, but allows us to measure what the results that we are getting. And if we're starting to achieve some of those outcomes that we talked about. Um, the outcomes vary, as you say, whanau to wau family to family, um, in particular places.

But fundamentally, we are looking for greater tho those whanau to have greater agency over their own decision making. Um, and we also see a lot of the government targets and things like that measured in outcomes. We will look, you know, we, we'll see greater, um, more children turning up to skill through these initiatives.

We'll see greater, um, GP enrollment, which will lead to, uh, reductions in emergency department, those sorts of things. But fra do you want to go into more depth about Yeah. Uh, I think the point Alistair made about perfection not being the enemy of the good, um, is, is useful here. Um, what we're trying is to simplify the measurement of social services.

Um, and we've started by, uh, setting out, um, and social investment fund ministers have agreed the high level outcome areas for the fund. Um, those are, for example, uh, improved health, greater safety, ensuring stable and secure housing, um, and improved not improving knowledge and skills, growing income and wealth and supporting people into work.

Those are our high level outcome statements for the fund that shouldn't ta shouldn't change, um, very rapidly, um, or very frequently. Uh, and then, uh, we would propose to measure those through a very small number of indicators that tell us something about progress.

So for, uh, reduced serious health events, you would look at, um, acute hospitalizations, uh, in the last 12 months, um, for, um, um, say for safety. You'd want to know about count of, uh, violent offense, um, offenses and police proceedings.

Uh, those tell us about the trajectories of the cohorts. Um, but, but most importantly, uh, what providers and families themselves count as progress will be able to be connected in the IDI with these measures. And over time we will understand the connection between those qualitative measurements through to those big outcome statements.

So we've got a, a long-term, um, long-term investment portfolio, and we can start to connect the data, say, um, what, what is it that we are wanting to achieve? What is it that providers are seeing in their own data? And then how does that link towards those, uh, slightly bigger population measures of success. Mm-hmm.

That's the big challenge, isn't it? It's actually identifying what a measure of success looks in terms of an outcome. I think, you know, hear I'm saying in terms of, um, what, you know, hanau see ASCE success as well, that's important as well. It's also identifying what there is and how to measure against that, um, could be a bit of a challenge as well. This will always be a ative process as we get more data into the IDI, but also as we learn more about what these, um, successes look like, we want to, a large part of social investment is taking learning loops.

So working with those providers who are working with those whanau to understand what actually really worked on the ground. We can have all the theory we like in Wellington, but if it doesn't work on the ground with those providers, um, and if it's not measurable, we can't, we need to be able to change t and have confidence if we are getting, if we need to be able to provide confidence to ministers that we are delivering what we expect to do.

And if not, we need to be able to flag that with ministers early enough that there's a chance to change tech that needs to be robust and transparent.
